items.q: What does it mean for an allele to be 'dominant'?
items.correct: It is expressed in the phenotype when at least one copy is present (in a heterozygote).
items.misconception: A dominant allele is the most common allele in a population.
items.random_wrong: A dominant allele physically destroys the recessive allele in the genome.
items.why: The everyday meaning of 'dominant' (most prevalent, strongest) misleads students into thinking it relates to frequency or power.

items.q: What does a gene code for?
items.correct: Typically a polypeptide (protein) — through transcription to mRNA and translation by ribosomes.
items.misconception: A gene codes for a specific physical feature like eye colour directly.
items.random_wrong: Each gene codes for an organ or body part as a whole.
items.why: Students learn 'gene for eye colour' shorthand, then think genes directly produce traits rather than producing proteins that contribute to traits.

items.q: Why is the secondary immune response to a pathogen faster and stronger than the primary?
items.correct: Memory cells generated during the primary response can rapidly proliferate and produce large amounts of specific antibody on re-exposure.
items.misconception: The pathogen becomes weaker after the primary exposure, so the body has an easier job.
items.random_wrong: The body produces a different, more powerful antibody during the secondary response.
items.why: Students attribute the difference to the pathogen rather than to memory cells (B and T memory lymphocytes), missing the role of immunological memory.

items.q: Does a 'dominant' allele mean it is the most common in the population?
items.correct: No — dominant means its effect shows when at least one copy is present; it is unrelated to how common the allele is.
items.misconception: A dominant allele is the most common allele in a population.
items.random_wrong: A dominant allele destroys the recessive allele in the cell.
items.why: Students link dominance to frequency, but a dominant allele can be rare; dominance only describes which allele is expressed in a heterozygote.

items.q: If both parents show a dominant trait, is it impossible for a child to show the recessive trait?
items.correct: No — if both parents are heterozygous (e.g. Bb), each can pass on a recessive allele, giving a bb child.
items.misconception: Two parents with the dominant trait cannot have a recessive-trait child.
items.random_wrong: A child can only show a trait that at least one parent shows.
items.why: Students forget hidden recessive alleles; heterozygous parents carry but don't show the recessive allele, which can combine in a child.

items.q: Two heterozygous brown-eyed parents cross (Bb × Bb). What is the expected phenotype ratio?
items.why: Correct answer: 3:1 brown : blue. “1:1 brown : blue” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: One dominant, one recessive (heterozygous). “Two dominant alleles” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In a Bb × bb cross, what percentage of offspring are expected to show the recessive phenotype (bb)?
items.why: Exact answer: 50. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
